inclusive_learning_strategies: Introduction to the Integrated Unit:Language Arts and

Science: Discussion & Exploration StationsThe teacher will show learners large pictures of weather symbols (sun, rain, wind, clouds) and ask them to name each one. The teacher will ask the learners to look outside and tell if they see what the pictures show. The teacher will ask the learners if the sky is always like that. Then, the teacher will inform the learners that they will be learning about the weather. The teacher will ask the learners the question ‘Is the weather important?'Afterwards, in small groups, the learners will be rotated in four named weather stations; Sun, Rain, Clouds, Wind, upon

a signal from the teacher. At each station, the learners will explore through observation and hands-on, some weather associated props and materials. For example; in the sunny station, may have sunglasses, visors and sun hats. The rainy station may have umbrellas, rain boots, raincoats and a simulated puddle. The cloudy station may have a cloudy sky collage with white or gray cotton balls or small pieces of soft fabric which learners can help complete by gluing on more cloud formations. The windy station may have learners play a game among their group members where they would have to use their mouths to blow some light weight items, such as strips of paper or leaves across a table, and the child who blows it the farthest wins.

After the station activities, the teacher will gather all the learners and they will be asked, ‘Which was their favourite weather station?' Why was that their favourite station? Is that also their favourite kind of weather?
Exit

Ticket: The learners will be asked to draw to show one way the weather is important. The learners will be helped to write a sentence about their picture and they will be encouraged to explain their picture to the teacher. To further enhance the children's learning, the learners might share their ideas verbally with a partner.
Language Arts, Science and
Art: Weather Walk

The learners will be taken on a short walk outside of the classroom. They can either go for a walk around the school or to the playground. The learners will be asked to use their different senses; sight, hearing, touch, and smell while on the walk. For example; they will feel the temperature outside or the grass/ plants, see the clouds or dry or wet areas or people and animals outside, listen to sounds like the wind or rain. Then the learners can return inside and discuss all their observations by saying how the weather affects them and others, as well as, what they may need to wear or do differently based on the conditions outside.The learners will be given canvases or white blank paper along with their water paints and paint brushes, to paint a picture of outside to include people, or animals or plants.

Science: Picture Discussion The teacher will give each learner a picture at random illustrating a weather symbol, such as; sun, rain, clouds and wind. The teacher should ask the learners what does the picture make them think of? The teacher will explain to learners that the weather is what we feel and see outside at the time we are looking out, like if it is sunny, rainy/stormy, windy or cloudy. After, the learners will be shown two large pictures of a Sunny Day and a Rainy Day (shown below) which will be posted on the board. Then, focusing on one picture at time, the learners will engage in a THINK-PAIR-SHARE activity, where they would describe the pictures by stating what is happening, what are they wearing, etc. (Please consult the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for explanation of the Think Pair Share strategy).

Then, the teacher will engage learners in a follow-up discussion of why the weather is important by asking them to think and share some other things they do with their families on a sunny day and on a rainy day.Sunny Day SampleRainy Day SampleThe learners will be asked to vote for their favourite picture by sitting or standing. Then they will help the teacher count the seated and the standing votes. The picture with more votes will remain posted on the board, while the other will be taken down. The teacher will ask learners to help identify all the people in the picture, and as they do, a small sticky note will be placed on each person in the picture. The learners will be told that they will be counting the number of people in the picture to determine the quantity. They will be informed that they will start from the one, point to each sticker and read aloud to ensure they have all the objects in the group. The quantity will be written on the board and learners will

read the number aloud. The same will be repeated for every group of objects in the picture, such as tress or animals or things. Then as an extension, especially to more advanced learners, the teacher can ask learners to identify which group has the most, or the least or the same amount.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Language Arts, Science and Craft: Circle TimeThe teacher dresses up in a weather appropriate attire, e.g. for rainy/stormy weather: a raincoat, hat, water boots and an umbrella for Circle Time and discussion. Then, the teacher will ask the learners the following questions to evoke thinking about the weather; e.g. What am I wearing? Have you or anyone else you know dresses like this? Do we always dress like this? When are we not dressed like this? What other ways do you dress when going outside/outdoors?After learners share their responses, the teacher will explain that the weather affects many parts of our lives, just like the clothes we wear. Learners will be asked, ‘Is the weather important?' Why is the weather important? The teacher will make notes of learners' free responses. After, the learners will select materials from a large table placed in the front of the classroom in order to create one item that can be used outdoors for a weather condition, then show their craft work with the class.Sample List of Materials:
Wikki Stix
Playdough
Popsicle sticks
Glue
Construction paper
Ribbon
Yarn
Scissors
Language Arts and Social

Studies: Weather GameThe teacher will ask learners to tell various activities which they do for various weather conditions. Afterwards, the learners will be told that they will be playing a game, and for this game they will be placed in small groups and be given one sheet of blank paper and a pencil for drawing. Learners will be told to choose someone in the group to draw on behalf of the group, and they can change that person if they wish after each round. The game will require learners to draw an item in response to each round of questions asked by the teacher based on things we do or wear because of the weather:Round 1: If it is raining, what can we use to stay dry?Round 2: When it is sunny, what do we wear to protect our eyes?Round 3: What is big enough to hide the sun in the sky?Round 4: What can be eaten and melts quickly in your hands, on a sunny dayRound 5: What is something children like to do outside on a windy day?A Sports variation of the game can be done by learners being placed in teams and participate in a relay or tag race (indoor/ outdoor) to find the items placed at a distance in front of them for each round of questions posed by the teacher. The teacher has the choice to change the questions listed above to coincide with the activity or items presented. Learners will be encouraged to cheer for each of their team members.
Language Arts, Mathematics and Social

Studies: Observation, Record and Colouring ActivityNB: This activity should be done over at least two consecutive days.The teacher will ask the learners to recite the days of the week and to state which day of the week is today. inclusive_learning_strategies: End of Week

One: Slideshow Presentation Science, Social Studies and Language ArtsThe focus of this ILS is to return to the question of the week: Is the weather important to me and my family?The teacher will use pictures on a slideshow to reinforce the importance of weather to learners, from prior class discussions on the topic. Since the depictions on the slideshow would have been familiar to learners, the teacher would ask the learners to talk about them, and discuss if this kind of weather is important to them and their family.After, the learners will be asked to create a drawing of a weather scene. For example, a rainy day at home, a sunny day at the beach or a windy day at the park. Learners will use materials such as crayons, paints and coloured paper. Learners will write a sentence about their drawing, explaining how that weather is important for them and their family. For those who have difficulty writing, the teacher will either provide the learner with a model or act as a scribe for the learner. Part of their weather wheel will include an arrow to point to the type of weather needed. Their observations will be shared to the whole class, where they would need to state the different kinds of weather.
Sample of a weather wheelSocial Studies and

Music: Weather DanceThe teacher will play instrumental music and ask learners to dance freely around the room, expressing different types of weather through movement. Learners can move like they're walking in the rain; stomping their feet in puddles of water as they dance, or swirl their bodies like the wind, or behave like sunbeams. The teacher will provide learners with props like umbrellas, sun hats and scarves to enhance the movements and add fun. Then, learners can select their own instruments to play along, such as tambourines to mimic rain or tapping drums to mimic thunder. Afterwards, learners will be invited to share the sounds that they made or heard and state the type of weather associated with those sounds.Social Studies and ArtsThe teacher will explain to learners that today they will be creating paintings inspired by different types of weather. To

engage a discussion, the teacher will ask learners the following questions:1. What does sunny weather look like?2. How can we represent windy weather in our paintings?3. What colours might we use for a rainy day?Learners will be encouraged to use their imagination to come up with ideas for their paintings. Using large sheets of paper and paints, learners will choose a type of weather and start their artwork. When the paintings are completed, learners will write their names on their art pieces and hang them on the wall in the classroom. The learners will put up an art exhibition, where the teacher will invite another class to view the learners' work, and the learners would get an opportunity to describe their art.
Language Arts, Social Studies and

Science: Seasonal ChangesThe teacher will use floor tape to divide the class into half (two groups) to represent our two seasons: wet season and dry season. Give one half of the class cut-outs sunglasses and the other half of the class mini cut-outs umbrellas. The learners will be asked why they think the classroom is set up in this way. Then, the teacher will also ask the learners if they have ever noticed that sometimes it rains a lot, while other times it does not rain much at all. The teacher will explain the result of two seasons with different types of weather.The teacher should introduce the idea of "weather pattern" and explain that there is a pattern of sunny days, or a pattern of rainy days, depending on the season. The weather pattern repeats every year, with the season. One season is wet,

which the umbrella group represents, and the other season is dry, which the sunglasses group represents.The teacher will draw a line halfway down the whiteboard or blackboard, and label one side wet season and the other side dry season using drawn examples of the same symbols as an umbrella and sunglasses to help distinguish the labels for the learners. Learners will be asked:What do you think happens during the wet season?How is the dry season different to the wet season?What types of activities do people do during each season? Learners' responses will be recorded by the teacher in the appropriate column on the board.The teacher might want to show the video about Wet and Dry Seasons to the class (see below in Additional Resources).The teacher will tell the children the time (month) that the wet and dry seasons usually begin in their region.

Science: Weather PatternsThe teacher will ask learners to name the different types of weather that they know. They will discuss briefly what each type of weather looks like. The teacher will explain to learners that they will be learning about patterns using pictures of different types of weather. The teacher will explain that a pattern is something that repeats in a predictable way.Before attempting to use the weather types pictures, the teacher will use the learners to show patterns such as; boy-girl, boy-girl, boy-..... and ask them who comes next: boy or girl. Then another example on a white board/ blackboard to illustrate patterns with two colour shades like; red-yellow, red-yellow, red-....... (Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for additional ways to help children learn about Patterning).

Afterwards, learners will attempt together patterns using the weather type pictures before being asked to do it independently.Using weather cards learners will complete a simple patternFor example:IMG:47:602:343

Science: Outdoor Picnic / DiscussionThe teacher will organize an outdoor picnic with the learners. It could be under the shade of a large tree near the school, or in a nearby park. Learners will be asked to act like police detectives and observe their surroundings through sight, touch and smells; such as dry patches of grass and other areas with fresh green grass or plants, animals feeding, muddy areas if any, puddles if any, dry and dusty ground, movement of leaves on trees and movement of the sea or a river if it can be seen from a distance.The teacher will ask the learners the essential question for the week: How does the weather make a difference to the land and water around us? Learners will explore the area in a safe way while sharing their observations.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies (Extension to Outdoor Picnic Activity): Shared Writing The teacher will have learners gather in a circle and discuss the outdoor picnic. The learners will be asked to share their favourite parts of the picnic and their observations about the weather. The teacher will write down on either chart paper or board, some of the keywords the learners mention about the picnic and the weather. The learners will be encouraged to think about:
What the weather was like (sunny, windy, cloudy, etc.)
How the weather felt (hot, cold, warm, etc.,)

Activities they did while outside because of the weather (collected leaves, jumped over puddles of water, played a game, etc.) During that time, the teacher will inform the learners that they will be writing a short story together about their outdoor picnic, by using the keywords from the discussion to complete sentences that are given and read by the teacher. The emphasis on keywords should include the high frequence/sight words for the week (jump, little, help, my) and the emphasis on the sounds (/m/ as in ‘my', and /h/ as in ‘help')

inclusive_learning_strategies: The teacher should extend this activity by having learners do some "first hand" science experiments on the ground or in spaces outside. Learners can choose a particular place outside, pretend that it is raining by using a water bottle, and then talk about what changes happened to the ground or the space. The learners will return to the class and further try to explain the ideas of the water puddles outside. The learners can act like scientists and draw what happened to the water puddles. The teacher can point out that scientists observe carefully and they also record what they observe, often with words and pictures.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Language Arts, Science, and Music - Mr. Sun SongThe teacher will play a video with the lyrics on the screen for learners to learn and sing along to the song: ‘Oh, Mr. Sun'Oh, Mr. Sun | Nursery Rhymes | Classic | Little Fox | Animated Songs for Kids (youtube.com)The teacher should have learners get into positions that they feel comfortable while learning the song. The learners can clap their hands or stamp their feet in time with the rhythm of the song.After performing the song a few times, the teacher and the learners will discuss each line of the song. The learners will say what they understand by each line. The learners will be asked, Do they like the sun? Do the land and rivers/sea like

the sun? During that discussion the teacher can help learners understand the positive and negative impact of the sun.This is a song that the children could sing every day when the class fill in the daily weather chart (see Week Two), if the weather is sunny. If the weather is rainy, the class could sing The Itsy Bitsy Spider song
Language Arts, Science and Social

Studies: A Nature Walk -The teacher will explain to learners that they will be taking a weather walk around the school to explore the land and to carry along paper and pencils for drawing. (The teacher might have the papers clipped to a piece of cardboard so it's easier for the children to handle). The learners will be asked to describe the type of weather outside. The teacher will explain that they will be using their senses; touch, smell, sight, and hearing to look out for ways that the land and water around them are changed by the weather. The teacher can start by giving an example so the learners will understand what they are expected to do. The learners can record their observations through drawing.The teacher can use the example of how a wet rock might change if the sun is shining on it. Firstly, the learners will observe or draw the dry rock. Then, the teacher will spray a bit of water on the rock, and the learners will watch to see how the sun can change the wet rock into a dry rock. The learners can also draw their observations showing this effect.
The learners can discuss their observations/ drawings in class in a follow-up lesson.
Language Arts, Science and Social Studies: Weather Experiments
The teacher will set up stations outside the classroom where simple experiments on the effects of the weather will be performed and discussed by the learners. For example:
Station 1: What changes happens to a rock when it rains, and then what changes happens to the rock after it is left a while in the sun? (NB: Sprinkle water and observe what happens)
Station 2: What changes happen to the soil when it rains, and what changes happen to a puddle when it is sunny? (NB: Sprinkle lots of water and observe what happens)
Station 3: What changes happen to water in a large bowl if it is windy? (NB: Use a powerful fan, or hair-dryer for demonstration and observe what happens)

Station 4: What changes happen to a mountain when it rains heavily and for a long time? (NB: Learners will pour cups of water down a model clay mountain and observe what happens)After the station experiments, the learners will state which station was their favourite and why. They will also say if those were the changes they expected to happen or if before today they have seen those changes happen. Then, the learners can give an idea of a station experiment to observe changes that can happen because of the weather.
Science & Language

Arts: Plant Observation/ Show and TellThe teacher would have asked learners before class to each bring in a potted plant. The learners will get turns to show and talk about their plants to the class. They can name their plants, describe the colours, where it is grown/ or found, and how they think their plants grow. After the learners have had their turns, the teacher will ask them what they think is their plant's favourite weather to grow, and also how the weather conditions help their plants grow.Then, the learners will each draw their plant and write a sentence with the help of the teacher about what kind of weather conditions help their plant to grow.

Science: Field Trip to a Local Farm-The learners will visit a local farm where the farmer will show and speak with them about how the weather helps his plants (fields) and animals. The farmer will explain how the rain, sun, and wind help make a difference to the plants and animals on his farm. The farmer can also share ways he conserves or uses the rainwater during the dry season, so the learners can understand the importance of the weather. The learners can come prepared to ask questions to the farmer.
Language
Arts: Shared Writing (Extension to Field Trip Activity)Sight Words
Emphasis: my, helpLetter Sounds
Emphasis: /h/, /m/

The teacher will explain to the learners that they will be making a thank you card together. The learners and the teacher will discuss the importance of saying thank you, and the teacher will inform them that they need to thank the farmer for the field trip. The teacher will help the children to remember what they saw and did on the trip to the farm. The teacher will ask learners what they would like to say on the card and write down those ideas on a chart or board. The teacher will choose the most popular ideas and begin writing on the chart or board. The teacher should find a way to incorporate the sight words: my, help; into the writing.For example:As each sentence is written the learners will help the teacher find the words; my & help. After the words are pointed out, the learners will read the sentences together with the teacher. The teacher will also ask learners to identify where they hear the sounds; /h/ and /m/ in the words being read. The learners will practice saying those sounds with the help of the teacher and give other examples of words where they hear those sounds. Some of those words will be written on a separate side of the chart or board by the teacher.Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for ideas to help children develop their recognition

Science: Sunlight Exploration-The teacher will use a large globe and a large flashlight to help support this lesson. The teacher can begin by asking the learners what is sunlight. Also, why is sunlight important? The teacher will show the learners a globe and explain what it is. Then, the teacher will use the flashlight to simulate sunlight shining on different parts of the globe. This can be done by spinning the globe and holding the flashlight steadily as the different parts are shown on the globe. The teacher will explain to the learners the effects of the sunlight in providing light and warmth to things on the earth which causes changes and makes some things grow. The learners can also take turns trying out the simulation activity with the flashlight and the globe or map. The teacher can also have the learners to pretend to be some of those things on the earth that the sunlight gives light and warmth and have them act it out. For example, the learners can pretend to be a forest and start as small as seeds or small plants and as the flashlight shines on them, they grow tall by standing up and opening their arms like branches on trees. Also, the learners can be taken outside to feel the warmth of the sunlight on different things; such as the soil, pavement, rocks, buildings, and plants/ grass.

Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies : Weather Role Play The teacher will begin by asking learners the question for the week: How does the weather make a difference to the land and water around us? Then, the teacher will explain to the learners that they will be in groups to discuss this and act out how their type of weather can affect the land and water around them. The teacher will divide the class into four groups and give them costumes or props representing four different weather conditions (sun, rain, clouds, wind) NB: Each group will represent and play out a different weather condition: Group 1: Rain Group 2: Sun Group 3: Wind Group 4: Clouds

inclusive_learning_strategies: After the learners are placed in their groups, the teacher with the help of learners through probing questions, will briefly explain what each weather condition does:Rain
Falls from the clouds, waters the plants, helps the plants to grow, creates water puddles on the ground, and fills the rivers and seas.Sun
Shines in the sky, makes the ground feel hot/ warm, helps the plants to grow, and dries up water on the ground.Wind
Blows across the land, rivers, and the sea, it can move the clouds and branches or leaves on trees and can make waves on the sea.Clouds

Can block the sun to prevent it from shining brightly, can make the ground feel cool, and can bring rain.Learners will act out each scenario one group at a time. Then there will a discussion by the teacher and the learners about what happened to the land and water in each scenario. Questions such as the following can be asked by the teacher:
What did the sun do to the land/water puddles?
How did the rain help the plants?
What happened to the land and rivers when it rained?
How did the wind affect the sea?Then, the teacher will ask the learners the essential question: Is the weather important?

inclusive_learning_strategies: Introduction to Week Four: Language Arts, Science and Arts/Craft: Create Weather Journals The learners will be asked the question for the week: How do we know about the weather? Their responses will be recorded by the teacher for future reference throughout the week. Then the learners will be given sheets of white blank paper, two sheets of hard paper for front and back of a book, some crayons, stickers, glitter, glue, etc. They will be asked to use those things to create their own Weather Watch Journal. The teacher will be helping learners to put their journals together and show them completed examples of some weather journals. One way to know about the weather is to look outside and observe. The teacher will tell the children that they will act as weather watchers to observe and record daily weather patterns every day for Week Four of the unit. The learners will be asked to look outside each day and observe the weather. Discuss what they see and draw a picture in their weather journals which they created. Then write or circle which word for the weather type (sun, rain, wind, clouds) is most observed under their picture. Also, each page of their drawing must be labelled according to the day of the week being observed. At the end of the week, there will be a discussion on any weather patterns they notice, and be asked questions by the teacher, such as:
How many sunny /rainy days did we have?
What was the weather like most of the time?
How can the sky tell us about the weather?

inclusive_learning_strategies: Language Arts, Science, Social Studies and Mathematics: Outdoor Circle Time The learners will be taken outside to sit under some shade, where they can properly observe the weather. The teacher can begin a discussion by asking the learners, how they or their family know how to dress to go outside. The teacher will ask the learners to do the following to help with the discussion:
Look at the sky (not directly at the sun). Can the sky tell you about the weather? What do you see?
Look at the trees, the rocks, and the land. Can those things tell you about the weather? What do you see?

Feel the grass and how does your body feel? Does the grass feel wet or dry? Do you feel cold, hot, or warm? Can the way the grass feels or the way you feel tell you about the weather? Afterward, the learners will return inside and talk again about how they or their family know how to dress to go outside. The teacher may need to prompt for answers, if the children only say that they can look outside, or go outside to see what the weather is. The teacher may prompt about what a weather forecast is, and if the children know about a weather forecast being on the radio, or on the television, or on a website. Studies: The Weather Forecasts/Report The teacher will review with the learners how they can know about the weather. The teacher will ask the learners if they have ever heard or seen someone on the radio or television talk about the weather. The teacher and the learners will discuss this by mentioning the different things the person may say about the weather. Studies: Teacher Show and Tell The teacher will bring an Emergency Preparedness Kit to show learners. The kit will include; a flashlight, extra batteries, a first aid kit, water bottles, a blanket, a small portable radio, extra cash, some non-perishable food items, a waterproof bag to place valuables or important documents, and a change of clothes. Emergency Kit Prediction Activity Instead of showing the Emergency Kit immediately, start by placing it in a mystery bag or box and asking learners to predict what might be inside. To spark ideas, you might ask: "What items would we need if we couldn't get to a store or had to stay safe at home?" Encourage learners to think of different situations where they might need specific items (e.g., if the lights went out, if it was cold, etc.). Show the learners the items in the kit, one at a time. The learners will name each item from the emergency kit and say how those items help in an emergency.

inclusive_learning_strategies: there are different drills for different emergencies, such as those caused by the effects of severe weather. One drill can be done by a family at home, when the parents/caregivers help the children to learn about places at home that they can use as a safe place if there is a storm or hurricane. When families and children and people at a school practice what to do in an emergency, they are following the motto: Be Prepared; Not Scared. The teacher will also discuss the use of sirens as a warning mechanism due to severe weather conditions, The teacher will show the learners a picture of a siren and explain how it is used to alert people that it is time to evacuate or leave an area because there is a dangerous weather situation approaching. Studies: Be

Prepared: Video Presentation The teacher will ask the learners the question for the week: How do people protect themselves and their belongings from the weather? The learners who may have already memorized the motto by now will be asked to say it aloud: Be Prepared, Not Scared. The teacher will explain to the learners that they will be viewing a short video on severe weather preparedness (see the link below). https://youtu.be/aEkAzbM4_HU?si=fT7wRrw_2JOU-iry

From the video, the learners will identify all the preparedness tips they observed. The learners will be asked to give other ways people can protect themselves, their animals, or their belongings which were not included in the video. The teacher can help them come up with those tips. For example, trim branches on trees or cut them down, place pets inside, or let them loose if possible. Repair broken fences, if you live close to a river or sea, you may have to move away until the storm passes, and secure vehicles in garages or somewhere away from trees or electric poles. After, the teacher will ask the learners to draw one way they can help their family prepare for a storm or hurricane. The learners will show and talk about their drawings to the class.

Crafts: Building a ShelterThe teacher will ask the question for the week: How do people protect themselves and their belongings from the weather? The teacher will hold a discussion with the children about what might happen if there is too

much sunlight and heat on their bodies, or on some of their belongings.The teacher will explain that the children will be building a model of a shelter that is used to keep people, animals, and things safe from the sunlight.The learners can choose the type of shelter they will build and the reason for building it. Examples of shelters are houses, kennels, tents, schools, and churches.The learners will select materials from the teacher's Arts/Crafts table to create their shelters. The teacher is encouraged to let the children start to build their shelters, then stop the class. Ask all the children to step away from their work and tell about any problems they are having or how they have figured out how to solve a problem (e.g. to get the walls of their shelter to stand up).After this discussion, all the children may return to their building with some new ideas of how to solve any problems they might be having.When all the shelters are completed, the learners will label them with the help of the teacher and place them on display for other learners and teachers, as well as parents, to view.
